file_name: Modiri et al. - 2025 - Can AI models assist patients in screening for non-melanoma skin cancer Evaluating diagnostic accur.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used AI large language models (ChatGPT/GPT-4 and Gemini) to analyze clinical images of non-melanoma skin cancer (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and benign lesions to evaluate diagnostic accuracy, danger assessment, and treatment recommendations.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is labeled as a 'RESEARCH LETTER' and presents original experimental results evaluating the diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT and Gemini on 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive. It includes specific methods, quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity), statistical analysis (McNemar's test), and new data findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Can AI models assist patients in screening for non-melanoma skin cancer? Evaluating diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT and Gemini using clinical images
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT (GPT-4) and Gemini as patient-led screening tools for non-melanoma skin cancer using 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive. The models were assessed on initial diagnosis, danger assessment, and treatment recommendations for basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and benign lesions. ChatGPT achieved a mean accuracy of 66.0% for initial diagnosis, while Gemini achieved 68.0%, with both models showing improved performance when considering the top three differential diagnoses. ChatGPT significantly outperformed Gemini in danger assessment (61.0% vs. 8.0% accuracy) and showed higher accuracy in treatment recommendations (70.0% vs. 48.0%). Although ChatGPT demonstrated a slight edge in overall initial diagnosis, the authors concluded that current AI capabilities are insufficient for standalone diagnosis but may support initial lesion screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: 25 images used in the dataset of 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive; classified as malignant/NMSC.
25 images used in the dataset of 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive; classified as malignant/NMSC.
25 images used in the dataset of 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive; classified as benign.
25 images used in the dataset of 100 clinical images from the ISIC archive; classified as benign.
Composite category comprising basal cell carcinomas and squamous cell carcinomas (50 images total); used for danger assessment and treatment recommendation analysis.
Composite category comprising seborrheic keratoses and benign nevi (50 images total); used for danger assessment and treatment recommendation analysis.
Explicitly excluded from the study to simplify the diagnostic task, as noted in the limitations section.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, benign nevus) and binary danger assessment (malignant vs. benign) and binary treatment recommendation (intervention vs. reassurance).
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates the diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT and Gemini in classifying clinical images into specific lesion types (multiclass classification for initial diagnosis) and binary categories for danger assessment (potentially malignant vs. likely benign) and treatment recommendations. Accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were calculated for these discrete class predictions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public clinical image archive from which 100 clinical images were randomly selected. The selection was filtered by 'clinical: overview' and 'clinical: close up' to mirror a patient-focused setting.

use.testing: Used as the input dataset for evaluating the diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT (GPT-4) and Gemini models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity between ChatGPT and Gemini models for initial diagnosis, danger assessment, and treatment recommendations.
To estimate the uncertainty of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for both ChatGPT and Gemini models.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in diagnostic accuracy (or sensitivity/specificity) between ChatGPT and Gemini models.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in diagnostic accuracy (or sensitivity/specificity) between ChatGPT and Gemini models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using p-values from McNemar's test. For example, ChatGPT significantly outperformed Gemini in danger assessment (p < 0.001) and top diagnosis sensitivity/specificity (p < 0.001), while the difference in overall initial diagnosis accuracy was not significant (p = 0.760 for overall, p = 1.000 for NMSCs).

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.notes: ChatGPT significantly outperformed Gemini in danger assessment (p < 0.001) and top diagnosis sensitivity/specificity (p < 0.001). The difference in overall initial diagnosis accuracy was not significant (p = 0.760). Both models showed improved accuracy when considering top 3 differential diagnoses. ChatGPT achieved a mean accuracy of 66.0% for NMSC initial diagnosis and 68.0% for benign lesion initial diagnosis. Gemini achieved 68.0% for NMSC and 68.0% for benign lesion initial diagnosis. For treatment recommendations, ChatGPT showed higher accuracy in both malignant (94.0% vs 88.0%) and benign (46.0% vs 8.0%) lesions.

architecture_details.prompting_strategy: Three patient-centered questions: 1) What is this? 2) Is this dangerous? 3) Does this need treatment or can it be left alone? Initial diagnosis recorded without additional prompting; top 3 differential diagnoses and top diagnosis recorded with prompting.

training_details.details: The study evaluated pre-existing commercial AI models as black-box systems; no model training, fine-tuning, or hyperparameter optimization was performed.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Textual diagnosis, danger assessment, and treatment recommendation
classification_or_output_head.evaluation_metrics: Accuracy, sensitivity, specificity for classification tasks

conclusions: ChatGPT and Gemini demonstrated comparable performance in the initial diagnosis of non-melanoma skin cancers (NMSCs), with ChatGPT achieving 66.0% accuracy and Gemini 68.0%.
ChatGPT significantly outperformed Gemini in danger assessment for both malignant and benign lesions (p < 0.001).
ChatGPT showed higher accuracy than Gemini in treatment recommendations for both malignant and benign lesions, with an overall accuracy of 70.0% compared to Gemini's 48.0%.
Both models showed improved accuracy when considering the top 3 differential diagnoses, suggesting that pinpointing the exact diagnosis remains challenging for current AI models.

The diagnostic accuracy of ChatGPT and Gemini exceeded some reported metrics for primary care physicians, who have been found to have sensitivity and specificity as low as 0.45 and 0.16, respectively.
The use of clinical (non-dermatoscopic) images reflects a patient-led screening context but may limit diagnostic precision.
The study's limitations include a small sample size and the exclusion of intermediate lesions like dysplastic nevi, which simplifies the diagnostic task compared to real-world scenarios.
While ChatGPT and Gemini show promise in supporting dermatological diagnosis, their current capabilities are insufficient for standalone diagnosis but may have value in initial lesion screening.

Further research and refinement of these models, incorporating larger and more diverse datasets, are necessary before considering their recommended use to patients as an initial screening tool or integration into practice.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size (n=100 images), which may affect the generalizability and statistical power of the findings.
The dataset exclusively uses clinical (non-dermatoscopic) images to mirror a patient-led screening context, which intentionally limits diagnostic precision compared to dermatoscopic analysis.
Intermediate lesions, such as dysplastic nevi, were excluded from the study, simplifying the diagnostic task compared to real-world clinical scenarios.
The models were evaluated as black-box systems without training or fine-tuning, assessing only pre-existing commercial capabilities.

While ChatGPT significantly outperformed Gemini in danger assessment (p < 0.001) and top diagnosis sensitivity/specificity (p < 0.001), the difference in overall initial diagnosis accuracy was not statistically significant (p = 0.760).

Both models showed improved accuracy when considering the top 3 differential diagnoses (ChatGPT 90.0%, Gemini 72.0% for NMSCs), suggesting that pinpointing the exact diagnosis remains challenging for current AI.

The authors conclude that current AI capabilities are insufficient for standalone diagnosis but may have value in initial lesion screening, noting that performance exceeded some reported metrics for primary care physicians.
No external validation was performed; the study relies on a single retrospective evaluation of images from the ISIC archive.
